problem_summary: 2015 MCM/ICM Problem C (ICM, interdisciplinary): Human Capital Management. The fictional org ICM has 370 employees across 6 salary-defined position levels and faces churn (people leaving/retiring) that must be replaced. Teams were asked to build a model of the human-capital network and use it to (3) compute a 2-year recruiting+training budget, (4) analyze how raising the annual churn rate affects the org's ability to stay 80% staffed and at what cost, and (5) assess the HR-health impact of higher churn concentrated in middle management levels. This Outstanding paper (Team 37284) frames the whole problem as a dynamic, multi-layer network of people + positions and drives it with a simulation-plus-optimization loop.
models_used: Three-graph human-capital network: (a) a people-people friendship graph G=(V,E) carrying intimacy weights, (b) a 6-level position tree (senior manager down to administrative clerk/inexperienced employee, keyed to salary), and (c) a people-to-positions bipartite graph whose edge values are assignment costs (with a virtual node v8 = 'in training' and node states 0/1-6/N/D).

Two custom probabilistic state variables: loyalty alpha (probability of staying; initial alpha^(0)=F(culture c, expectation gap e)) and intimacy beta_ij (initial beta^(0)=F(time-known t_ij, interest similarity s_ij)). These are the model's core invention.
Loyalty transformation matrix T (m x m, t_ij = loyalty multiplier when moving level i->j, with composition rule t_ij = t_ik * t_kj for skipped promotions).

Matrix-form loyalty update across time using assignment indicator matrices A^(t): partial^(t) = partial^(t-1) * diag[A^(t-1) T A^(t)T] — a clean linear-algebra encoding of 'apply the promotion-transition gains only along each employee's actual move'.
Tenure-decay loyalty law alpha = alpha^(0) * d^floor(n/12 - mu): loyalty rises if time-in-position n is below the mean-promotion years mu, decays if stuck too long.
Intimacy-update law tied to position-gap change: beta_ij = beta_ij^(0) * (delta_l_old + 1)/(delta_l_new + 1) — gap widens => intimacy drops; gap closes => intimacy rises.

Two-channel churn simulation: (1) self-motivated churn with prob 1 - alpha_i, and (2) contagion churn from already-departed neighbors with prob P_i = sum_j (1 - alpha_i) * beta_ij, with a one-month influence lag so contagion propagates the following month.

Productivity model Y = sum lambda_i S_i n_i - sum sigma_i S_i |n_i - n_tilde_i|: a salary-proportional output term minus a structural-imbalance penalty (deviation of headcount n_i from optimal n_tilde_i per level).
Cost model split into C1 (structural/indirect cost = sum sigma_i S_i |n_i - n_tilde_i|) and C2 (direct cost = salary + recruitment r_i*n_i^new + training k_i*n_i^old).

Modified Genetic Algorithm for the position-assignment optimization: chromosome = a length-500 string of per-person position levels (alphabet 0/1-6/N/D), fitness = total cost, with promotion-only mutation and crossover and immediate elimination of chromosomes that violate per-level capacity (e.g. more than 25 people assigned to level 3).

Monte-Carlo computer simulation of churn+recruitment over 24 months, initialized with a Random Graph for the unknown friendship structure and random loyalty/intimacy values rescaled to hit the known 18% churn rate; results averaged over 5 runs.

Multi-layer network extension (information network + relationship network + position network) drawn from team-science literature (Salas; Stokols; Kivela et al. Multilayer Networks) as the closing generalization.
key_techniques: Inventing two named, defensible latent variables (loyalty, intimacy) to convert a vague HR problem into quantifiable churn probabilities — and crucially separating self-driven churn from peer-contagion churn, which directly answers the 'network' spirit of the problem.

Decomposing the system into a clean three-part recurring loop: (1) loyalty/intimacy evolve as the network changes, (2) computer simulation drives churn + recruitment, (3) GA is the ONLY lever the HR manager controls — this 'only the optimizer is the human decision' framing gives the model crisp managerial meaning.

Encoding promotion effects as a transformation matrix and expressing the whole-population loyalty update as a single diag[A T A^T] matrix identity, then validating it with a fully worked 2-employee/4-position numeric example ([0.95,0.92] -> [0.9785,0.9384]).

Domain-tailoring a standard GA rather than using it off the shelf: promotion-only operators (no firing, no demotion), capacity-constraint feasibility check during selection, and a chromosome alphabet that natively represents 'under training' and 'departed' states.

Calibration discipline: tuning the random initial loyalty multiplier until the simulated churn (~19%) and recruitment (~12%) match the problem's stated 18%/10%, giving a credibility check before drawing conclusions.

Explicit parameter taxonomy in a dedicated Section 6: parameters fixed from data/experience (T, lambda_i, sigma_i, loyalty/intimacy) vs. parameters tuned by experiment (GA population N=200, iterations K=2000, mutation 0.4, crossover 0.1) — with convergence plots justifying each GA value.

Answering Task 4 with a churn-rate sweep table (21%-40%) plus a feasible/subordinate-feasible/infeasible region chart, surfacing the counter-intuitive but well-argued finding that direct cost falls with higher churn (lower salary bill) while indirect/structural cost rises.

Reframing Task 5 (mid-level churn) as a promotion-opportunity argument with a concrete level-by-level headcount flow table showing 66.7% and 53% fresh-promotion rates at levels 4 and 5, tying it back to the loyalty model.
why_it_won: From a judge's view this paper wins because it nails the ICM Problem C judging priorities better than the math sophistication alone would suggest. (1) It is fundamentally a NETWORK paper for a network problem — the three-graph + multi-layer formulation directly honors the 'fluid network of human capital' language in the prompt, where weaker teams just did headcount accounting. (2) The loyalty/intimacy abstraction is the kind of original, nameable modeling idea judges reward; it is simple, defensible, and load-bearing across all three tasks rather than decorative. (3) The simulation+GA architecture is coherent end-to-end and self-consistent: every quantity used in the tasks (churn, recruitment, cost split) traces back to the same engine, and they calibrated it against the given 18%/10% before trusting it. (4) It produces concrete, committed numbers for all three tasks (165.28 two-year budget; 617.64 vs 533.66 cost at 25% vs 35% churn; mid-level churn refreshes structure) instead of hedging. (5) It shows engineering maturity by customizing the GA to the domain (promotion-only, capacity-feasible) and by devoting a full section to parameter sensitivity with convergence evidence. (6) The counter-intuitive Task-4 result (higher churn lowers direct cost but raises structural cost) is exactly the kind of non-obvious, well-justified insight that separates Outstanding from Meritorious.
section_highlights: Structure is textbook-strong and easy for a judge to grade. The one-page summary cleanly previews all three networks, the dynamic loop, and the three task answers with their numbers up front. Section 2 lists 7 explicitly justified assumptions (each with a real-world rationale, e.g. 85% fill = max output, economic-man salary proxy, no firing) — this pre-empts judge objections. The build-up is incremental and signposted: Section 3 builds the three static graphs then fuses them; Section 4 adds the time stamp, the loyalty/intimacy dynamics, the churn simulation, the cost model, and the GA, in that dependency order. The worked numeric loyalty example (Figure 9 and the diag[A T A^T] computation) makes an abstract matrix formula concrete and checkable. Heavy, purposeful use of figures (20 figures: network diagrams, GA chromosome/operator illustrations, GA flowchart, churn-rate line charts, feasibility-region area chart) gives every claim a visual. Section 6 (parameter analysis) and Section 9 (honest strengths/weaknesses) signal rigor and self-awareness. Section 7's multi-layer/team-science extension shows reading beyond the prompt and gives a graceful generalization ending.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Almost everything is driven by self-assigned random initial values: loyalty, intimacy, the friendship graph (a Random Graph), and several coefficients (lambda_i, sigma_i, d, mu) have no data backing — the team admits this in Weakness 1. Conclusions are therefore qualitative-credible but not empirically grounded.
Functions are left as black boxes: alpha^(0)=F(c,e) and beta^(0)=F(t,s) are never given explicit forms, so the model is more of a framework than a fully specified computable system in places.

The bipartite/GA setup uses position LEVELS (6 buckets) as the V-set rather than the ~370 distinct seats, and capacity constraints (e.g. '25 positions in level 3') are asserted without showing the full per-level capacity vector or how it was derived from the provided org structure table.

They ignored the provided organizational chart ('does not coordinate with the structure table', Weakness 2), so the rich department/reporting structure — arguably central to an HR network — is dropped.
The productivity model Y is defined but never actually used: the team pivots to cost-minimization because output data is absent, leaving lambda_i and the whole Y equation as unused scaffolding.

Some numeric results are weakly explained: the Task-4 cost table is non-monotonic (e.g. dips and spikes across churn rates) and the narrative smooths over this as a clean 'rises then falls' story; the feasibility-region chart's underlying data looks schematic/idealized rather than simulation-derived.

English is non-native throughout (frequent grammar/typo issues, e.g. 'dos', 'synthetize', mislabeled figure captions where a mermaid 'flowchart' is actually a neural-net-looking diagram) — tolerated by judges but a polish gap.
One-month contagion lag and 'no two connected people churn the same month' is a convenient simplification that likely understates cascade/herd churn dynamics.
reusable_patterns: The 'three independent graphs then fuse into one network, then add a time stamp for dynamics' construction is a clean reusable template for any relational/network modeling problem.

Invent 1-2 named latent state variables (here loyalty + intimacy) that (a) have an intuitive real-world meaning, (b) carry an initial value F(known factors), and (c) have an explicit update law as the system evolves — this turns soft problems into quantitative ones and gives reviewers something memorable to grade.

Separate a probability into independent channels (self-driven vs. peer-contagion churn) and sum/combine them — directly demonstrates 'network thinking' and is reusable for adoption, default, infection, and influence problems.

Encode entity-level updates as matrix operations using a 0/1 assignment-indicator matrix A and a transition matrix T (state^(t) = state^(t-1) * diag[A^(t-1) T A^(t)T]), then validate with a tiny fully-worked numeric example — both rigorous and reader-friendly.

Build a simulation-plus-optimization loop where the optimizer (here a domain-customized GA) is explicitly framed as 'the only lever the decision-maker controls' — gives the model managerial interpretability.

Customize a standard metaheuristic to domain rules instead of using it generically: restrict operators (promotion-only mutation/crossover), use a state-rich chromosome alphabet (0/levels/N/D), and reject infeasible chromosomes during selection via capacity constraints.
Calibrate the simulator against a known baseline statistic from the problem data (tune a free parameter until simulated 19% churn ~ given 18%) before trusting any downstream result.

Devote a dedicated section to a parameter taxonomy (data/experience-determined vs. experiment-tuned) and justify each tunable with a convergence or sensitivity plot — strong, low-effort credibility booster.

Answer 'what if rate X changes' tasks with a parameter-sweep table plus a feasible/infeasible region map, and proactively surface a counter-intuitive trade-off (direct cost down, structural cost up) as the headline insight.
Close with a literature-grounded generalization (multi-layer networks, team science) that extends the model without needing new computation — a graceful, high-ceiling ending.
